[109:29]is all about drug trafficking.

[109:40]he calls narco-terrorism.

[109:42]That these Latin American countries traffic drugs like fentanyl and cocaine and other

[109:58]And specifically he's talking about fentanyl.

[110:02]Because theoretically, you could say that

[110:06]because fentanyl is so deadly at such small doses

[110:09]that it resembles something like a biological

[110:12]or chemical weapon.

[110:20]which theoretically you could make that case.

[110:50]into the United States.

[110:51]It is killing tens of thousands of people.

[112:05]They say, well, Venezuela traffics cocaine.

[112:08]And that is true.

[112:09]Venezuela is a somewhat major node in the international trafficking of cocaine, but

[112:16]Venezuela traffics almost all the cocaine that is produced or held in their territory

[112:22]in the West to Europe.

[112:25]Most of the cocaine that has moved eastward from Columbia into Venezuela is trafficked

[112:31]out to Europe.

[112:32]Not a lot of it makes its way into the United States.

[114:12]how the world works.

[114:14]This is how all interventions, all wars in the entire history of the world are sold,

[114:20]especially and in particular in the age of mass democracy.

[114:51]a country or a government's policies are based out of self-interest for the regime, if not

[115:08]terms.

[116:26]with this argument about oil.

[116:33]exporting countries of the world.

[116:35]Founding member of OPEC,

[116:38]the oil and petroleum exporting cartel.

[117:22]In terms of how much oil they produce every single day

[117:27]Venezuela is actually underperforming. They produce about 800,000 to a million barrels of oil per day

[117:36]For context, there's about 105 million barrels of oil produced in the world every day.

[117:43]So if there's a hundred and five million barrels per day being produced in the world and Venezuela accounts for

[117:50]800,000 to 1 million of those barrels that is less than 1% of

[117:57]Global oil production by far and away the biggest oil producers are the United States

[118:02]Saudi Arabia Russia Iran and they blow Venezuela out by 2010 five times that

[118:10]So, why is Venezuela attractive for oil?

[118:14]It's because they have the largest proven oil reserves.

[118:18]According to some estimates, they say it's 300 billion barrels of oil in Venezuela.

[118:25]According to other estimates by the US Geological Survey, it could be as much as a trillion

[118:30]barrels of oil. And so the question of Venezuela's oil is really in its untapped potential. There's

[118:36]going to be no immediate effect on Venezuelan oil production in the world. Specifically,

[118:43]let's think about it from the point of view of geopolitics.

[118:47]A lot of people said that the United States invaded Venezuela, either to one, deprive

[118:54]China of the oil they get from Venezuela, or two, and obviously this goes hand in hand,

[119:00]so that the United States can start receiving the oil that Venezuela produces.

[119:06]But again, you got to go back to the numbers.

[119:13]They only import about 600,000 of those barrels from Venezuela.

[119:17]So it's about 4%.

[119:19]So Venezuela makes less than 1% of the world's oil every day.

[119:24]China imports less than 4%.

[119:28]Less than 4% of their imports is coming from Venezuela.

[119:31]So, if the goal of the United States in taking Venezuela's leader and sees in control of

[119:37]the oil fields, if the goal was to have some immediate effect on either the global price

[119:44]of oil or on the supply of oil to China, this is going to have a negligible impact.

[119:53]Let's talk about on the side of the United States.

[120:00]The United States will sanction Venezuelan oil at will.

[120:04]Sometimes we buy it, sometimes we don't.

[120:06]And that's based on our political posture towards Venezuela,

[120:10]whether we're more aggressive or not.

[120:13]Under the first Trump administration,

[120:14]we put heavy sanctions on Venezuela's oil in 2019.

[120:19]We wouldn't buy it.

[120:20]When Biden got elected, he gave Venezuela a waiver

[120:22]from the sanctions and we started importing it.

[120:26]So we could always have access to Venezuela's oil

[120:29]and they would have been happy to sell it to us.

[120:31]Last year, our special envoy to Venezuela,

[120:34]Rick Grinnell, actually negotiated a deal

[120:36]where they would sell us their oil.

[120:38]They would give us even their critical mineral mines,

[120:41]their gold mines.

[120:43]We could make money from their existing mines

[120:45]and we would be able to take advantage of future prospects

[120:49]if we did a proper survey

[120:50]and found out the true mineral wealth of the country.

[121:00]it.

[121:01]It's not a question of whether we're depriving China of a particularly important source of

[121:22]What is the situation in Venezuela?

[121:26]It hasn't always been the case that they produced

[121:32]Back in the 1990s,

[121:34]they produced three and a half million barrels per day.

[121:36]But the reason it's gone down over the past 25 years

[121:42]They don't service their existing oil wells.

[121:45]They don't do what is necessary

[121:46]to maintain the infrastructure.

[121:53]They thought they might produce over 6 million barrels per day.

[121:56]And there's that, you know, number again, 270, you know, but at one time, 3.5 million,

[122:03]and they thought they could go, they could go up to 6 million.

[122:42]That being said, the timeline to take advantage of the resources is many, many years.

[122:49]It's an open-ended question if that is going to happen at all.

[122:53]Trump convened a meeting of oil executives this past week, and they all said that at

[122:58]this point in time, they will not be investing billions of dollars in Venezuela.

[123:03]And the reason being is because there's a very bad environment in the country for enforcing

[123:09]or arbitrating contracts.

[123:17]oil company has no recourse.

[123:18]They cannot enforce existing contracts.

[123:28]is the Venezuelan state will expropriate the assets of American oil companies without

[123:54]And that would be guaranteed by robust contract enforcement, international arbitration.

[124:05]It might be worked out in the future. Even if you're able to create an environment where

[124:11]contracts can be enforced, where there's international arbitration, the timeline to

[124:17]service or increase the infrastructure in Venezuela is years.

[124:25]There is one American oil company in Venezuela, Chevron.

[124:34]They say that on the early end, you're talking about in a year, two years, maybe Venezuela

[124:40]gets up to 1.5 million barrels per day.

[124:47]You get up another 500,000 barrels per day,

[124:50]five to 700,000 barrels per day,

[124:52]depending on the estimate for how much they make now.

[124:56]And again, this is not a significant amount

[124:58]of oil production to affect the global price of oil.

[125:02]It's not a significant amount of oil for the United States.

[125:06]The only relevant strategic factor with the oil,

[125:10]there's two things.

[125:12]It's about what kind of oil

[125:15]Venezuela's making and where the oil's going not with regard to China

[125:20]So first and foremost the kind of oil in Venezuela is what they call heavy sour crude oil

[125:29]Compared to light or sweet crude oil

[125:31]That talks about the chemistry of oil

[125:35]Heavy and light refers to how viscous and thick it is

[125:39]Sweet and sour refers to how much sulfur is in the oil.

[125:44]Why does this matter?

[125:45]It takes a different kind of infrastructure to process and refine heavy and sour oil versus light and sweet oil.

[125:54]The United States imports heavy and sour oil from Canada, from Mexico, and from Venezuela.

[126:01]In the 1990s, U.S. oil companies built a ton of refining infrastructure in the Gulf of Mexico and Texas and Louisiana, 18 refineries, because they anticipated that they would be getting this heavy, sour oil from Mexico and from Venezuela.

[126:18]Mexico was making tons of oil, Venezuela was making tons of oil.

[126:23]And so they made this bet that in perpetuity,

[126:26]the United States would be getting its oil

[126:28]from these countries.

[126:29]It would be this particular kind of oil.

[126:32]And that requires this infrastructure

[126:35]to process and refine this oil.

[126:38]And it's a very intensive process.

[126:40]What happens in the mid 2000s is that many

[126:43]of Mexico's oil wells run dry.

[126:45]And the amount of oil they produce

[126:47]actually goes down considerably.

[126:49]The same thing happens in Venezuela.

[126:52]So what happens to all these refineries in the Gulf of Mexico that were built up, cost

[126:56]billions of dollars, took many years?

[126:59]This is a major capital investment.

[127:02]What do you do if there is a supply crunch?

[127:05]You're just not getting the oil from these countries that you anticipated.

[127:09]Canada comes in.

[127:11]And Canada increases their oil production

[127:14]and starts exporting it to the United States.

[127:17]And now the United States imports about 60% of its oil

[127:20]from Canada, because this is the heavy, sour crude

[127:23]that we need in Texas and Louisiana

[127:26]that it is efficient for this infrastructure to refine.

[127:29]And we actually take the light, sweet crude

[127:31]that we get from the oil sands through fracking

[127:34]and we export that to other countries

[127:36]that have the appropriate infrastructure for that.

[127:41]If Venezuela increases the amount of oil production from 800,000 to 1.5 million barrels per day,

[127:49]this is not going to affect global oil prices.

[127:53]It's not going to be a major issue for China. They'll just get more oil from Russia.

[127:58]But that slight increase and then maybe projected future increase, it is going to affect Canada.

[128:06]Canada actually becomes one of the biggest losers.

[128:11]We, right now, are reliant on Canadian heavy sour oil.

[128:16]This is why we're building all these pipelines to get their oil from Canada down to the Gulf

[128:21]of Mexico.

[128:22]But it is cheaper to transport oil from Venezuela on a ship from Venezuela to Mexico to a port

[128:30]than it is to bring it down from a pipeline.

[128:33]So what does that mean?

[128:39]It's cheaper per barrel to transport that means that that puts downward pressure on the price of oil

[128:46]That we buy from Canada

[128:48]So the idea that Canada has leverage over our economy because of the kind of oil they make

[128:55]This might hurt them in the short and intermediate term

[128:58]So that's the first country that gets affected in terms of oil. The second country is Cuba

[129:04]Cuba needs about 100,000 barrels of oil per day.

[129:09]They get 30,000 barrels from Venezuela.

[129:14]And they get it almost for free.

[129:16]And they get it because Venezuela and Cuba

[129:19]are ideological and political allies.

[129:21]Cuba is a communist country.

[129:23]Venezuela is a socialist country.

[129:25]And so for decades, there has been a transfer

[129:27]between these two countries.

[129:29]Cuba provides human capital like doctors,

[129:33]intelligence agents, other things.

[129:36]Venezuela gives them free or discounted oil.

[129:39]One of Trump's first demands of the new Venezuelan regime

[129:43]is that they stop sending oil to Cuba.

[129:45]And Trump has implemented a blockade of Venezuelan oil.

[129:49]So, that is going to put immediate pressure on Cuba, regardless of how much oil Venezuela

[129:54]makes, whether it gets to China, and that's an open-ended question, how much of it goes

[130:00]to the United States, how much more they might make.

[130:03]These are all questions for a later date.

[130:06]But as of right now, like today and in the near term, Cuba is deprived of about a third

[130:12]of its oil imports.

[141:05]Is Venezuela a neoconservative intervention?

[141:09]Well, let's talk about it.

[141:11]So what did we do in Venezuela?

[141:14]Did we strictly speaking pursue a regime change policy in Venezuela?

[141:20]Did we change the regime?

[141:23]Some people might say the obvious answer is yes.

[141:26]I think I saw Michael Tracy and Glenn Greenwald.

[141:29]They said, no one can argue that Trump isn't in favor of regime change.

[141:33]But did we change the regime? What is a regime? What is the regime in Venezuela?

[141:39]The regime in Venezuela is called Chavista.

[141:43]That's the style and the form of the regime that was brought to power by Hugo Chavez when he was elected in 98 and

[141:50]with the Bolivarian Revolution in Venezuela.

[141:53]It's a socialist,

[141:55]gangster-ish, call to personality based on redistribution using their oil wealth to pay for social services.

[142:03]What else is the regime? It is not only the president, but it is their civilian leadership

[142:09]and it is the military leadership. It is the security forces. It is, for example, the very

[142:16]powerful defense minister and interior minister. Now ask yourself, here we are two weeks later.

[142:28]Because while Nicholas Maduro was kidnapped and removed from the country, the rest of

[142:33]the regime remains completely intact.

[142:36]It is totally still there.

[142:38]Maduro's vice president became the president.

[142:41]The Interior Minister remained, the Defense Minister remained,

[142:46]every government minister remained,

[142:48]and the security services remained.

[142:50]The security services that are loyal

[142:53]to this Chavista government, this socialist government,

[142:56]they all remained too.

[142:58]We didn't go in and arrest everybody.

[143:00]We didn't do reeducation.

[143:02]We didn't do a loyalty test.

[143:04]We didn't put on trial the whole leadership class

[143:08]or military leadership.

[143:10]One person was removed, and the second in line moved up.

[143:15]And you could say, well, what difference does it make?

[143:18]They took the president.

[143:21]We did not do this in Iraq.

[143:24]We did not do this in Syria.

[143:26]We did not do this in Afghanistan.

[143:28]We did not do this in any of those countries.

[143:32]When the United States invaded Iraq,

[143:34]we didn't just take out Saddam Hussein

[143:40]pick up the pieces.

[143:42]We took out Saddam.

[143:44]We tried to eradicate and debathify.

[143:47]That was the ruling one party in Iraq.

[143:50]We tried to debathify the entire country

[143:53]and turn it into a democracy with elections.

[144:03]we tried to change the regime.

[144:22]people. Same thing in Syria. Same thing in Libya.

[154:23]for the US intervention in Venezuela.

[154:26]And this gets back to that subtle difference.

[154:28]A lot of people said, this is a war under false pretenses

[154:34]because of this pretext about drug trafficking.

[154:38]A lot of people said this is neoconservatism, and so this is another useless war, an illegal

[154:45]war.

[154:45]They said it's contrary to international law or even constitutional law.

[154:50]All these arguments were given.

[154:53]Here is my unwavering axiom and why I would justify the intervention in Venezuela.

[155:01]It starts with America first, okay?

[155:06]So, the show is not called non-intervention, the show is not called isolation, it's not

[155:12]called UN, it's not called nationalism for all nations.

[155:17]Fuck the other nations and fuck the UN.

[155:21]It's called America first.

[155:24]Ideologically, I am a realist.

[155:26]What does that mean?

[155:28]It means the only imperative for any government, for any state is to put its own people first.

[155:35]What is the only imperative of a U.S. government if we have a government?

[155:40]It is to ensure the safety and security and continuity of the United States of America.

[155:46]If it can't do that, if it isn't doing that, then it is not doing its job.

[155:51]That is the moral imperative.

[155:53]Survival is the imperative.

[155:55]Because if a nation doesn't survive, well, then it cannot do anything else, just like a human being.

[156:02]My imperative as a human being is survival.

[156:05]I need to eat, I need to drink water,

[156:07]I need to defend myself if I'm under attack,

[156:10]I need to secure myself.

[156:12]And it goes beyond, if someone is pointing a gun at me,

[156:16]I shoot them first.

[156:18]We have houses with locks on our doors.

[156:21]We have situational awareness.

[156:23]We take precautions.

[156:25]Because as long as there are other people

[156:28]that we share the world with,

[156:30]They threaten us by their very existence as long as there are other individuals that want

[156:36]the things that we want, whether it's resources, whatever, that they want more, we want more.

[156:43]It creates a security dilemma.

[156:45]As long as I am a man and there's another man and we go to embrace and shake hands,

[156:51]I'm worried he's got a rock behind his back.

[156:56]If another man has a weapon, I need a weapon.

[156:59]If I think he might have a weapon, he might be able to kill me.

[157:02]And if he can, he might, because he's thinking the same thing.

[157:06]And he thinks it might be safer to go and kill me first on the off-end chance and I'm plotting

[157:12]to kill him than to wait and take the chance.

[157:15]This is called a security dilemma.

[157:17]And so as long as there are governments,

[157:20]as long as there are states,

[157:21]they are in a ceaseless security dilemma.

[157:24]They're in a ceaseless calculation

[157:27]about how to secure their survival.

[157:30]And a nation is more complex than a person.

[157:32]A nation needs a lot of things.

[157:34]It needs an idea to keep all of the people together.

[157:37]A nation needs resources for a complex modern economy.

[157:41]It needs energy.

[157:42]It needs food.

[157:43]It needs certain minerals.

[157:46]It needs a lot of things.

[157:48]And so what is involved in US foreign policy

[157:52]and military policy is the first question,

[157:55]is this in favor of the continuity

[157:57]and survival of the American nation?

[157:59]And it gets to be quite convoluted.

[158:02]We are a great power.

[158:03]We exist on the world stage among other countries

[158:06]that would like to be world powers.

[158:08]They wanna be world powers

[158:10]because they wanna be optimally secure.

[158:14]They want to be in the position of advantage.

[158:17]They're working to secure resources, territory.

[158:20]They want to have that primacy, that comfort of knowing that if it came down to it, they

[158:25]could win a conflict.

[158:27]And so, like I said, it gets to be quite convoluted.

[158:30]A lot of people like to say, well, America's a republic.

[158:33]If we're not attacked, then we shouldn't go and fight anybody.

[158:36]Well, America has interests pursuant to its survival that go beyond whether we're directly

[158:43]attacked.

[158:44]You know, this is what happens at Pearl Harbor.

[158:47]Japan is worried that we have this powerful Navy.

[158:50]We are watching these developments in Europe and Asia.

[158:53]They're worried that if they make some moves to secure oil, we might intervene.

[158:57]They launch a preemptive attack.

[158:59]We need to be making these chess calculations.

[159:03]We need to think one, two, three, four, five moves ahead.

[159:05]And we need to think about everything that a nation needs.

[159:11]guns and ships, but everything that a nation might need, pharmaceuticals, food, fertilizer.

[159:17]And we need to think about, in a hypothetical matchup, in a hypothetical war, how are we

[159:23]going to best be able to defend ourselves or launch a preemptive attack?

[159:27]And then we have to look at geography.

[159:29]This is the basis of geopolitics.

[159:32]So the show is called America First.

[159:34]I am a realist because first and foremost, I believe our government should be looking

[159:40]If you have a US passport, if you have US citizenship,

[159:44]if you're a tax-paying American and you don't want to die,

[159:48]you want to secure resources and safety

[159:51]and all the things that a government

[159:53]can secure for its people,

[159:55]you want our country to be as powerful as possible.

[159:58]You want our country to look

[160:00]after our vital strategic interests.

[160:03]And so under that premise,

[160:05]I believe that yes, sometimes war is justified.

[160:08]Yes, interventions,

[160:09]some small all the way up to a total war are necessary.

[160:15]It is necessary to have nuclear weapons.

[160:17]It is necessary to have a giant military, military bases.

[160:21]It is necessary to have these things actually.

[160:23]I'm in favor of those things

[160:24]as long as they are for survival, continuity,

[160:28]preservation of Americans.

[160:31]This has actually always been our doctrine

[160:34]at least it was from the very beginning.

[160:36]And when our founders set up the government,

[160:40]they took note of this,

[160:41]they acknowledged that our greatest asset

[160:43]as a fledgling republic long before we industrialized,

[160:47]before we had a navy, before we had an army,

[160:50]before we were any kind of a power.

[160:53]Forget about a global power,

[160:54]before we were even a regional power, a local power.

[160:57]The founding fathers knew that our great strategic asset

[161:01]is that we are protected by the oceans.

[161:05]We don't have to concern ourselves with what is happening in Europe or Asia, you know,

[161:10]Russia, the concert of powers in Europe, Napoleon, because they're never going to come all the

[161:16]way over here and invade us.

[161:17]And if they do, we have an advantage.

[161:19]They have to cross an ocean and that involves logistics and resupply and it's a difficult

[161:25]journey.

[161:25]We're right here.

[161:27]And so the founding fathers said, we should not go out into the world looking for monsters

[161:34]We don't need to.

[161:36]We're blessed by the geography.

[161:38]We don't need to actually concern ourselves with the great power conflicts that are happening

[161:42]over there and the intrigue and the diplomacy and sometimes the wars.

[161:47]And they said, we should not pursue these types of alliances where we get entangled in

[161:53]them and we're committed permanently to other countries.

[161:57]Why would we?

[161:58]We're safe.

[162:01]Shortly after the country comes into existence, you get the promulgation of what is called

[162:05]the Monroe Doctrine by James Monroe, written by his personnel, John Quincy Adams.

[162:13]And this is delivered at the State of the Union.

[162:16]And I'll read it to you.

[162:17]This is in 1823.

[162:19]President James Monroe says, quote,

[162:22]the occasion has been judged proper for asserting

[162:24]as a principle in which the rights and interests

[162:27]of the United States are involved,

[162:29]that the American continents by free

[162:31]and independent condition, which they have assumed

[162:34]and maintain, are henceforth not to be considered

[162:37]as subjects for future colonization by any European powers.

[162:42]This goes back literally 200 years.

[162:45]You might have heard of them in Roe Doctrine.

[162:47]Written by John Quincy Adams,

[162:50]stated by President James Monroe,

[162:53]he says,

[162:55]no European countries can colonize the Americas.

[162:58]And by the way, this is said,

[163:01]when America does not even have the ability to enforce it.

[163:05]At this time, the United States

[163:07]is not any kind of military power.

[163:10]And look at the territory of the United States

[163:12]at this point.

[163:13]This is before the Mexican session.

[163:14]This is before a lot of the territorial gains.

[163:17]We don't have a navy, we don't have an army.

[163:19]We can enforce this against Spain, against France,

[163:23]against the United Kingdom, against Russia.

[163:26]But it's a statement of principles.

[163:28]It's aspirational.

[163:30]It says that it's in our best interest

[163:33]that these European countries don't colonize the Americas.

[163:36]That's the only thing that can threaten us.

[163:38]Well, after the Civil War, the United States industrializes.

[163:43]After the Civil War, we lay down the railroad, we build factories, and we build the Navy.

[163:49]Our industrial might translates to wealth.

[163:52]Our productive capacity leads to wealth.

[163:55]Wealth leads to military power, specifically, naval power.

[163:59]And suddenly, we have the ability to enforce the Monroe Doctrine.

[164:03]And then we revise it.

[164:06]And so the secretary of state, Richard Olney,

[164:09]for the president Grover Cleveland in 1895,

[164:12]modifies the Monroe Doctrine.

[164:14]They call this the only interpretation, the only O-L-N-E-Y.

[164:19]This is the corollary to the Monroe Doctrine.

[164:22]Now that we have a Navy,

[164:23]and now that we could actually do something,

[164:25]this is what he says.

[164:26]He reinterprets it.

[164:28]At the turn of the last century, he says, quote,

[164:31]today the United States is practically sovereign

[164:36]and its fiat, its decree, is law upon the subjects to which it confines its interposition.

[164:44]Why? It is not because of the pure friendship or goodwill felt for it.

[164:50]It is not simply by reason of its high character as a civilized state,

[164:54]nor because wisdom, justice, and equity are the invariable characteristics of the dealings of the United States,

[165:00]It is because in addition to all other grounds,

[165:03]its infinite resources combined with its isolated position

[165:07]render it master of the situation

[165:09]and practically invulnerable as against any

[165:13]or all other powers.

[165:15]So what he's saying is we can do whatever we want.

[165:19]We are the most powerful country.

[165:22]We have infinite wealth.

[165:23]We're protected by the oceans.

[165:25]Nobody can challenge us.

[165:26]And so it is a matter of fact that what we say goes.

[165:30]because who can tell us otherwise?

[165:33]And this is then the basis for the Spanish-American war

[165:36]where we fight to take Cuba from Spain

[165:40]and we take the Philippines and Puerto Rico.

[165:42]And this is then the basis with the Roosevelt Coralie

[165:45]to intervene in all the banana republics in the Caribbean.

[165:47]And then this is the basis for the Truman Doctrine

[165:49]in the 40s and 50s to intervene in Central and Latin America

[165:53]against communist dictatorships.

[165:57]I will add, and this is sort of an interesting note,

[166:01]where does the phrase America first come from?

[166:04]Well, maybe it's first historical iteration

[166:08]is the America first committee,

[166:10]which formed up in the interwar period,

[166:12]shortly before the United States entered World War II.

[166:16]And the America first committee was a broad coalition.

[166:20]There were liberals, communists, leftists,

[166:22]but they're all so fascist, rightist,

[166:24]German-American booned.

[166:27]Lots of different people came together in the America First Committee to oppose US entry into World War two specifically the war in Europe

[166:36]But also the war in the Pacific and

[166:39]What was their argument?

[166:41]They said that even if the Nazis win it doesn't matter. We're safe

[166:45]They said it does not matter what the outcome of the war is in World War two if the Axis wins will be fine

[166:51]They're not going to invade us. They can't

[166:55]The original America First Committee, Henry Ford was a member, Charles Lindberg was a member, JFK's father was a sympathizer.

[167:04]This is what they wrote about the Monroe Doctrine. They said, quote, this is a speech by Charles Lindberg to the America First Committee in 1941.

[167:14]He said, there is a policy open to this nation

[167:17]that will lead to success.

[167:19]A policy that leaves us free to follow our own way of life,

[167:22]develop our own civilization.

[167:24]It is not a new and untried idea.

[167:27]It was advocated by Washington.

[167:29]It was incorporated in the Monroe Doctrine.

[167:32]Under its guidance, the United States

[167:34]became the greatest nation in the world.

[167:36]It is based upon the belief that the security of a nation lies in the strengthened character

[167:41]of its own people and recommends the maintenance of armed forces sufficient to defend this

[167:46]hemisphere from attack by any combination of foreign powers.

[167:50]It is a policy not of isolation, but of independence, not of defeat, but of courage.

[167:55]It is a policy that led this nation to success during the most trying years of our history.

[168:04]So even the original America first committee was not against them in road doctrine, not

[168:10]against hemispheric defense.

[168:12]Even they said, we don't want to fight in Europe.

[168:15]We don't want to fight in Asia, but fighting in the Western hemisphere.

[168:17]Well, that is what Americans do.

[168:19]Well, we've always done.

[168:22]We did it in the Mexican-American war.

[168:24]We did it in the Spanish-American war.

[168:26]We intervened all over the place.

[168:28]And as long as we have the requisite force

[168:30]to defend the hemisphere from any country

[168:32]or combination of countries,

[168:34]we'll be okay.

[168:34]We don't need to worry about the others.

[168:37]And so let's get into that strategic interest.

[168:41]What specifically does this mean?

[168:45]Practically for us.

[168:47]Why did the United States intervene in Cuba in the 1890s?

[168:52]Why did they intervene again in the 1950s and in the 1960s?

[168:57]Why did they intervene in some of these Central American countries?

[169:01]It is because a large volume of trade moves through the Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean Sea.

[169:11]Something like 40% in terms of volume, not dollar value of American shipping goes through

[169:17]the Gulf of Mexico.

[169:18]And it goes through narrow choke points between Mexico and Cuba, between Cuba and Florida.

[169:24]How far is Cuba from Florida?

[169:26]It's about 90 miles.

[169:28]People have swam that distance.

[169:32]If there is a rival power in Cuba, in Venezuela, in Mexico, in any of these countries, they

[169:41]threatened American shipping.

[169:43]They could potentially deploy weapon systems

[169:47]in reach of the United States.

[169:48]And obviously that happened in 1962

[169:52]during the Cuban Missile Crisis

[169:54]when Communist Cuba after the failure of the Bay of Pigs

[169:58]asked the Soviet Union for missiles to protect itself.

[170:03]And Nikita Khrushchev deployed nuclear missiles to Cuba within reach of the United States.

[170:09]And this is the most important 13 days in the Cold War.

[170:14]The Soviet Union put weapons systems 90 miles away from Florida that can carry weapons of

[170:20]mass destruction.

[170:22]This is why it is in our strategic interest.

[170:26]Russia is far away, China is far away, Europe, the Middle East, all of that is far away.

[170:32]This is our backyard.

[170:33]I don't think people realize.

[170:35]The Caribbean is our backyard.

[170:37]That is where American shipping goes through.

[170:40]That is where a trans shipment occurs from the Atlantic to the Pacific Ocean through

[170:44]the Panama Canal.

[170:47]And all of these countries are a stone's throw away

[170:50]from the United States.

[170:53]What has been happening in Latin America

[170:55]for the past 25 years?

[170:58]Well, ever since China entered the World Trade Organization

[171:01]in 2001, they have made a concerted effort

[171:06]to impose their will in Latin America.

[171:10]And so what are they doing?

[171:11]They have enrolled the majority of Latin American countries

[171:14]in the Belt and Road Initiative.

[171:16]So, they're building infrastructure in Latin American countries, infrastructure like what?

[171:22]Deep water ports.

[171:24]They're building ports in Jamaica, in Mexico, in Cuba, in Venezuela, in Peru, in Ecuador,

[171:31]in Argentina.

[171:32]They're building ports.

[171:34]Why does that matter?

[171:36]The same deep water ports that can harbor, let's say, some of the largest commercial

[171:46]What we are talking about shipping, they carry the most shipping containers, the same ports

[171:50]that can handle that can handle a People's Liberation Navy vessel.

[171:56]And what can you carry even on a commercial shipping vessel?

[172:01]You can carry a containerized missile system.

[172:04]What China has invented is a shipping container that contains an intermediate range missile

[172:12]And it looks like a shipping container.

[172:14]It looks like any shipping container you see on a truck, on a barge, and inside of

[172:19]a concealed is a missile launch platform.

[172:21]You can put those on a commercial ship.

[172:24]And so there have been studies done by the Center for Strategic International Studies.

[172:30]They say that there are up to 38 ports.

[172:33]China has all over the Caribbean, all over Latin America.

[172:39]It's infrastructure that can be used for commercial purposes, but also have the capability to

[172:45]have military purposes also.

[172:48]In terms of data collection, power projection, they could theoretically have a military

[172:53]vessel there, 38 of them, and some of them strategically placed in the Caribbean.

[173:00]And if you look at what is happening over in China, everybody knows that the clock is

[173:21]Why does that matter?

[173:35]We have effectively boxed China in controlling islands making it so that the only way China

[174:17]once they're wide open in the Pacific, who's to say they don't deploy strategic assets

[174:24]Have an enormous amount of debt to China.

[174:27]China is a partner or have an interest in these infrastructure projects.

[174:32]These infrastructure projects can be used against the United States, whether it's ports,

[174:37]it could also be radar dishes, space station, some of it acts as actually sovereign soil.

[174:54]The immediate loser, the immediate effect of the intervention in Venezuela is Cuba.

[175:06]This is part of a shorter term domino theory approach to break the back of some of our

[175:13]worst adversaries in the Caribbean and Latin America.

[175:22]Or World War four, however you count them,

[175:24]we are currently engaged in a great power struggle

[175:28]with China.

[175:29]That's what all of this is about.

[175:31]And what the United States is doing

[175:32]is getting its strategic house in order.

[175:35]We have neglected Latin America for 25 years.

[175:39]The United States has allowed China to gain a foothold

[175:41]in large part because we gave up.

[175:53]We're not going to intervene in these countries.

[175:56]And so basically while we were sleeping or while we were involved in Israel's wars,

[176:01]we let a dragon into our hemisphere.

[176:05]This is our fortress.

[176:07]This is our home base.

[176:09]This is all we got to worry about.

[176:14]That's where our trade occurs.

[176:16]That's where our supply chains are located.

[176:18]That's where our shipping happens.

[176:20]These are the important waterways.

[176:22]This is the important territory.

[176:24]This is what is proximate to us in our neighborhood.

[176:27]And while we were focused all the way over in Iraq

[176:30]and Afghanistan building a democracy,

[176:33]China was engaging in a trade offensive,

[176:36]an infrastructure and political offensive

[176:39]in these countries, peeling them away from us,

[176:50]it as a foothold against the United States.

[176:53]You don't have to worry about China if they are constrained within the first island chain.

[176:58]But they have global ambitions and they just released another white paper on Latin America.

[177:04]And it doesn't take a rocket scientist to figure out what the end game is with these

[177:09]infrastructure projects that have dual use.

[177:12]You think this is benevolent?

[177:14]You think this is charitable?

[177:16]You think this is ideological?

[177:18]Like everything it is strategic.

[177:20]And so if you are America first, then you understand that the US government, its first

[177:26]and only priority is to ensure the survival of the United States.

[177:30]We have to secure our vital strategic interests, which are geographical, which are the waterways

[177:36]and territory close to the United States.

[177:38]We want to prevent these weapons systems from getting too close to us, whether they be containerized

[177:50]From getting information on us through these ports, radar dishes, whatever.

[177:54]We want to be prepared for the future of strategic competition with China.

[177:59]And so for that reason, I support this.

[178:03]I support the Newman road doctrine.

[178:05]What's more, it's cheap, it's quick.

[178:08]We got what we wanted.

[178:10]We got out.

[178:16]And I said, in the buildup to this operation, I said, I don't support an invasion of Venezuela.

[178:23]Venezuela is a big country, 29 million people.

[178:26]It's like about the size of Texas.

[178:28]The terrain is difficult. You have a lot of cities, a lot of jungle terrain. I don't think

[178:33]it's a good idea to put 100,000 troops in Venezuela in these guerrilla battles, fighting

[178:39]the remnant of the Maduro regime. I don't think that's a good idea. I don't even really

[178:44]think regime change is a good idea. I said last year, what I supported was the Rick

[178:52]Rick Grinnell worked out a deal where Maduro would remain but he would give us their oil,

[178:57]give us their gold, change the laws, reorient from Beijing and Moscow to Washington.

[179:03]I said, we should just take the deal, forget about the military intervention, just take

[179:08]the deal.

[179:09]A military intervention will be costly, time consuming, absorb energy.

[179:13]It's uncertain whenever you roll the dice like that.

[179:21]which is we are doing the deal.

[179:24]Washington is working with this Venezuelan regime,

[179:27]this vice president, Delcey Rodriguez is visiting.

[179:30]There isn't this immediate impetus

[179:32]for a democratic transition.

[179:33]We just wanna work with them on these strategic interests.

[179:37]And you know what?

[179:39]Taking swift, decisive military action,

[179:42]if that puts the fear of God in these other countries,

[179:45]if a demonstration of force reinforces deterrence,

[179:50]diminishes miscalculations, incentivizes friends to cooperate, enemies to stand down, then

[179:57]I think that it's absolutely worth it. And you got to keep in mind, you know, a lot of

[180:01]people, they memorized these anti-war arguments. They said, war is always bad. Anything the

[180:07]government does is bad. It's all bad. It's all Iraq.

[180:12]Iraq was a ground war with 300,000 soldiers.

[180:16]It's still going on today.

[180:17]25 years cost us $3 trillion.

[180:20]This operation lasted two and a half hours.

[180:23]It maybe cost a billion dollars, okay?

[180:28]And do the math on that.

[180:30]One billion versus three trillion.

[180:33]I saw somebody today say,

[180:34]it's costing us $9 million a day.

[180:37]Do the math.

[180:39]Iraq cost us 3,000 times more.

[180:43]This cost us nothing.

[180:45]Okay, we sent $3.8 billion to Israel.

[180:47]It doesn't affect us.

[180:48]This is a $1 billion operation.

[180:50]It lasted two and a half hours.

[180:52]If it carries with it some benefit for the United States,

[180:56]pressure on Canada, pressure on Cuba, reinforced deterrence,

[181:01]Put China on notice. And here's another thing. This operation took place when the Chinese delegation was negotiating with the Maduro regime.

[181:09]I don't know if that was an accident.

[181:11]Trump seems to do this all the time. He bombed Syria when the Chinese were at Mar-a-Lago, kidnapped Maduro when they're in Caracas.

[181:18]It puts them on notice too.

[181:21]And so I'm totally in favor of it, unless of course it goes off the rails.

[181:26]And Trump did say there's not a lot of certainty here about what comes next.

[181:31]Initially, Trump said we will directly administer Venezuela.

[181:37]I don't like the sound of that.

[181:49]And that is just classic gunboat diplomacy.

[181:52]We get them to do what we want, stop sending oil to Cuba,

[181:56]send less of it to China, more of it to us,

[181:58]let ExxonMobil come in or whatever,

[182:01]then I'm in favor of it.

[182:03]And I think that it's easily defensible

[182:05]under America first, but as I said before,

[182:08]The problem is, so many of you people, and I'm speaking to many of you, but in general,

[182:15]so many people that have come to criticize Israel, they never let go of their ideological

[182:20]priors.

[182:22]There's the last thing I'll say, then we'll get into super chats.

[182:25]Last thing I'll say is this, so many people got involved.

[182:30]You got red-pilled last year, you got red-pilled two years ago.

[182:34]I've been doing this for 10 years.

[182:38]Some people got red-pilled a year ago and they were liberal normies, disgruntled liberal normies.

[182:45]And why did they come over?

[182:47]They said, Israel's committing a genocide.

[182:49]That's true.

[182:51]It's barbaric.

[182:52]It has to do with Jewish supremacy.

[182:54]But of course, the real problem with America is that we have a Jewish oligarchy that is making us pay for it.

[183:01]They couldn't do it without us paying for it.

[183:08]It's from an America first axiom.

[183:11]You say, how can a country put its people first if it has this parasitic class, if it

[183:18]has this fifth column of people that are loyal to another state and seem to be operating

[183:27]to rise through the ranks, gain power in the United States, and then use our country for the benefit

[183:32]of some other country or for the Jewish community at large. But many people that got involved

[183:38]are simply being manipulated by, as I said, ideological communists, liberals, Islamists.

[183:44]Muslims hate Israel because their homeboys are being killed. They hate America, too.

[183:55]And, you know, he's a nice guy. I've talked to him before. I don't hate the guy, but

[183:59]he says to Sneeko, you know, the West has terrorized the world for 200 years. They need

[184:05]to be knocked down a peg.
